steps.title: Set up the comparison
steps.content: The claim is not just that e-books became more popular over time. It is that e-books were more commercially successful for straight-through fiction genres than for nonfiction genres that readers use by flipping back and forth. The data should therefore compare one fiction genre with one nonfiction reference-style genre, preferably in the same year.

steps.title: Reject same-category checks
steps.content:
• Choice B compares 2006 romance sales with 2016 romance sales, so it shows change within one fiction genre.
  • Choice C compares romance with science fiction and fantasy, but both are fiction genres meant to be read straight through.
  • Choice D compares 2011 travel guide sales with 2016 travel guide sales, so it shows change within one nonfiction genre.

steps.title: Compare fiction to reference
steps.content: Cookbooks are nonfiction books that readers often consult out of order, while science fiction and fantasy books are story-based works typically read from beginning to end. In 2016, e-books were 10.5% of cookbook sales but 36.7% of science fiction and fantasy sales, directly illustrating the claimed contrast. The answer is A.
